text: The surgeon opens the mesentery at the level of the sacral promontory, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the low anterior resection procedure.
The surgical field shows significant edema in the tissue, with a visibly thickened peritoneum, indicative of post-radiation changes.

The surgeon releases adhesions between the anterior abdominal wall, omentum, small bowel, and a portion of the transverse colon at the previous incision site. Initial laparoscopic ports are placed in the left hypochondrium, followed by transitioning to the conventional port positions.

The surgeon creates a gap in the peritoneum and extends it towards the root of the inferior mesenteric artery. The dissection progresses beyond this point to the lower border of the pancreas, where the inferior mesenteric vein is dissected and ligated.

The surgeon continues to dissect the mesentery, gradually exposing the inferior mesenteric artery amidst the surrounding adipose tissue and edema. The artery becomes visible as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon carefully dissects close to the inferior mesenteric bundle to avoid nerve damage, preserving the nerves while progressing towards the pelvis. The dissection continues under the vascular bundle and mesocolon to expose the ureters clearly before ligating the inferior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon exposes the gonadal vein, visible as a blue structure beneath the surgical instrument. The ureters are positioned more medially, and the fascia covering the gonadal vein is clean, indicating the dissection is in the correct avascular plane.
The surgeon dissects the left ureter, visible as a white structure, with small vessels running on top. The gonadal vein is seen just lateral to the ureter.
The surgeon continues dissection cranially to expose the inferior mesenteric vein located just below the lower border of the pancreas, preparing for a high ligation of the vein.
The surgeon opens and dissects the peritoneum over the inferior mesenteric vein, revealing the vein clearly as it is manipulated with surgical instruments.

The surgeon clips and cuts the inferior mesenteric vein just below the lower border of the pancreas. Attention is then turned to the dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery, where lymph nodes from the artery's origin at the aorta are dissected and removed for histopathology. The surgeon further removes lymphovascular tissue surrounding the left colic branch of the inferior mesenteric artery, preserving the white structure of the left colic artery and its vascular sheath.

The surgeon removes lymphovascular tissue surrounding the inferior mesenteric artery and its branches for histopathological examination. The vascular sheath is opened to expose the superior rectal and sigmoid arteries, creating a plane between these vessels for ligation and cutting.

The surgeon hooks and dissects the sigmoidal branch of the inferior mesenteric artery, creating a plane above the instrument. The superior rectal artery is clearly dissected, and additional space is created for ligation and cutting, allowing for secure placement of clips proximally and distally. The surgeon continues to create space for the sigmoidal artery to ensure secure clipping and cutting.

The surgeon identifies the branches of the inferior mesenteric artery, beginning with the ligation of the superior rectal artery using two proximal clips and one distal clip, with visible pulsation in the vessel lumen. The sigmoidal branch is then ligated with one distal clip and two proximal clips, clearly showing the vessel lumen between the clips.

The surgeon applies a clip to the dissected portion of the inferior mesenteric vein and cuts it, aiming to reduce tension and weight on the mesentery. The vascular pedicle is used for traction, visibly opening up the tissue plane.

The surgeon continues dissection between the fascia propria of the rectum and the presacral fascia, revealing the hypogastric nerves and sacral vein. As the dissection progresses caudally, the plane opens easily, aided by edema from prior radiotherapy. Sharp dissection is performed with minimal bleeding, preserving the tissue planes. The dissection extends beyond the previously dissected gonadal vein.

The surgeon enters the lateral wall from the medial dissection, revealing the psoas muscle gradually appearing laterally. Below the preserved perinephric fat and Gerota's fascia, the surgeon meticulously enters the avascular plane.

The surgeon identifies the perinephric pad of fat below the instrument, noting its distinct color and appearance compared to mesocolonic fat. The mesocolon is elevated, and dissection proceeds from the medial to the lateral side. Small adherent fibers are sharply dissected using ultrasonic energy for better lymphatic sealing, particularly important in patients who have undergone pre-operative chemo radiotherapy.

The surgeon completes the dissection from the medial side towards the lateral side, focusing on the lateral attachment of the mesocolon. The lateral attachments are divided along the line of Toldt, visible as an avascular plane, allowing for quick and efficient division.

The surgeon continues to divide the lateral attachments of the colon, progressing towards the splenic flexure. The splenic flexure is fully mobilized, ensuring adequate bowel length for a tension-free anastomosis in the low anterior resection procedure.

The surgeon takes down the remaining posterolateral attachments near the splenic flexure, fully mobilizing the colon. The fixed portion of the colon becomes completely free and mobile. The dissection then continues towards the pelvis, with careful identification and preservation of the ureters.

The surgeon points out the tumor near the peritoneal reflection using an instrument, noting the suspicion of serosal involvement. Due to the patient's obesity and visceral fat, identifying the correct planes is challenging. The surgeon proceeds to take down the remaining posterior attachments while carefully identifying and preserving the hypogastric nerves. The lateral division of the peritoneal attachments is extended, maintaining a safe distance from the suspected tumor infiltration to ensure a negative circumferential resection margin (CRM). Inflammatory fluid is visible, attributed to post-radiotherapy effects.
The surgeon provides additional clearance in the surgical area, ensuring the fold is retracted sufficiently to prevent injury to the bladder or prostate during the laparoscopic procedure.

The surgeon continues the dissection anteriorly, deliberately staying above the Denonvilliers fascia due to the tumor's anterolateral location. The fascia is taken along with the specimens to ensure additional clearance, which is crucial for patients with anterior tumor growth.

The surgeon identifies a white shiny structure below the dissecting instrument, which is the denonvillous fascia. The fascia is divided to enter the right mesorectal plane. The surgeon also takes down some lateral attachments, likely including the middle rectal artery and pelvic splanchnic nerves, referred to as the lateral ligament.
The surgeon identifies the right seminal vesicle on the lateral wall and carefully maintains a safe distance from it while entering the correct surgical plane.

The surgeon divides a few more adherent fibers posteriorly, following the technique of dissecting posteriorly before moving laterally and anteriorly to preserve vital structures and facilitate easier identification of the surgical plane.

The surgeon observes inflammatory fluid collecting in the hollow of the sacrum while performing a cylindrical dissection. The shiny mesorectum is preserved, and the avascular plane is visible with minimal bleeding.

The surgeon reaches the pelvic floor, revealing white tendinous structures identified as the levator plate and the pubic oxygen ligament. The tumor is located a few centimeters above the peritoneal reflection, allowing for adequate distal margin without opening the levator plate.

The surgeon dissects and divides anterior fibers, revealing the white shiny denonvillous fascia below the instrument. The seminal vesicle is visible above the instrument, with the prostate seen just above, indicating work is being done below the prostate level.

The surgeon divides the attachment of the Denonvilliers fascia, re-entering the correct plane of the mesorectum. The left seminal vesicle is visible on the anterior wall above the instrument, while small branches are divided. The levator ani muscle is seen beyond the instrument and below it.

The surgeon completes the dissection, revealing the assistant's finger inserted per rectally, which moves to indicate that the dissection has reached the level of the levator ani muscle on the pelvic floor.

The surgeon uses intraoperative colonoscopy to identify the level of the tumor and the area for mesorectal division. The mesorectal excision is performed with a six-centimeter margin below the tumor's lower border, ensuring a tumor-specific approach.
The surgeon clears the mesorectum, ensuring it is sufficiently exposed and bare to facilitate effective stapling of the rectum. The mesorectum and mesocolon remain intact without any breach.
The surgeon uses an articulating stapler with significant angulation to apply it effectively in the narrow space of the patient's pelvis, following complete clearance of the mesorectum.

The surgeon displays the stapled and cut specimen edge, making the stapler straight for removal. The last portion, primarily composed of fat, is clipped and cut, freeing the entire specimen. The dissected pelvis is visible, with the ureter and prostate clearly seen anteriorly. A good total mesorectal excision (TME) is evident, with no excess fat posteriorly or laterally. The anastomosis is performed using a 31mm EEA DST stapler from Covidien, followed by a saline leak test.
